body_txt: When a TV show or film is exported around the world, every country has its own dub in the country's language, with a different cast of voice actors. But sometimes, it could happen that one or several of the actors are able to speak in multiple languages. If this is the case, they might reprise their role from the original version in other dubs too. Alternatively, the character they play doesn't talk much, so learning their lines in other languages will allow them to preserve their role without adding too much to production time. The benefit of this practice is that because the same actor is providing the voice, they can draw on their on-set motivations and experiences to deliver a performance as close to the original as a dub can get. Compare with Non-Dubbed Grunts (where the original voice is kept for sounds that are not actual lines), The Song Remains the Same (where a song is left in the original language) and Role Reprise (where an actor does the same role in multiple works). A real-world form of Bilingual Dialogue .
